edges.description: Defective ClC-K/barttin channel function in Henle-loop epithelia impairs epithelial salt transport, feeding into the renal NaCl reabsorption defect.
edges.target: Impaired NaCl Reabsorption in Thick Ascending Limb

edges.description: The same type 4 ClC-K/barttin channel system is required in the inner ear; barttin mutations impair potassium secretion in the stria vascularis and vestibular labyrinth, while dual ClC-K channel mutations impair inner-ear channel function.

edges.source: Impaired NaCl Reabsorption in Thick Ascending Limb
edges.target: Compensatory Distal Sodium Reabsorption and Potassium Wasting

nodes.description: A subset of Bartter syndrome patients develop chronic kidney disease over time, likely due to nephrocalcinosis, chronic hypokalemia, recurrent dehydration episodes, and long-term NSAID use. In a Korean multicenter study, 11% developed CKD G3-G5.

evidence.snippet: Despite management, a significant portion of this population exhibited growth impairment, while 11% developed CKD G3-G5.

Three patients developed chronic renal failure: one with KCNJ1 mutations during the second decade of age and two with CLCNKB and BSND mutations and without nephrocalcinosis during the first year of life.

nodes.description: In type 4 Bartter syndrome, BSND loss-of-function or digenic CLCNKA/CLCNKB disruption impairs the ClC-K/barttin chloride-channel system shared by renal epithelia and the inner ear. This links the renal salt-wasting branch to the sensorineural-hearing branch without implying that all Bartter subtypes cause deafness.

evidence.reference_title: Bartter syndrome: causes, diagnosis, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the BSND gene cause type IVa BS and result in impairment of barttin insertion in the plasma membrane of CLC-Kb and CLC-Ka channels in the Henle’s loop as well as in the inner ear, which interfere with epithelial salt transport.

evidence.reference_title: Bartter syndrome: causes, diagnosis, and treatment.
evidence.snippet: type IVb is a digenic disorder with mutations in both CLCNKA and CLCNKB genes, which can lead to impairment in the functioning of two chloride channels and as a result, severe salt wasting and deafness.

nodes.description: Reduced NaCl reabsorption in the TAL increases sodium delivery to the distal convoluted tubule and collecting duct. Enhanced sodium reabsorption through ENaC in principal cells, driven by secondary hyperaldosteronism, creates a lumen-negative potential that drives potassium and hydrogen ion secretion, producing hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis.
nodes.id: Compensatory Distal Sodium Reabsorption and Potassium Wasting

evidence.snippet: patients with BS present hyperreninemic hyperaldosteronism with normal/low blood pressure, reduced peripheral resistance, and hyporesponsiveness to antihypertensives.

nodes.description: Recurrent episodes of dehydration from salt and water wasting, especially in infancy.

evidence.snippet: Postnatally newborns suffer from recurrent episodes of severe dehydration and electrolyte imbalance which can lead to fatal outcome.

nodes.description: Poor weight gain and growth failure from chronic electrolyte depletion and volume contraction.

evidence.snippet: This syndrome is associated with an increased antenatal and neonatal mortality because many patients fail to thrive.

nodes.description: Increased urinary calcium excretion due to loss of the lumen-positive potential driving paracellular calcium reabsorption in the TAL. Prominent in Types 1 and 2.

evidence.snippet: all the ten hypercalciuric patients with aBS had nephrocalcinosis at the time of diagnosis and the last visit

nodes.description: Elevated urinary prostaglandin E2 excretion, particularly in antenatal forms. Contributes to fever, diarrhea, and vascular symptoms.

evidence.snippet: severe salt wasting, hyposthenuria, increased production of PGE2, and failure to thrive

nodes.description: Renal potassium wasting due to enhanced distal K+ secretion driven by increased sodium delivery and hyperaldosteronism.

evidence.snippet: BS is characterized by polyuria, failure to thrive, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, hyperreninemia, and hyperaldosteronism.

nodes.description: The biochemical hallmark of Bartter syndrome: persistent hypokalemia with hypochloremia and metabolic alkalosis resulting from renal potassium and chloride wasting.

evidence.snippet: Bartter syndrome (BS) is currently recognized as a rare inherited renal tubular disorder that affects around 1 in 1,000,000 of the population, caused by a defective salt reabsorption in the thick ascending limb (TAL) of loop of Henle, resulting in salt wasting, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis with relatively low levels of serum chloride.

nodes.description: Renal magnesium wasting from impaired paracellular magnesium reabsorption in the TAL.

evidence.snippet: Henle’s loop has another important role in the reabsorption of divalent cations such as calcium (Ca2+) and magnesium (Mg2+), which are reabsorbed by a passive paracellular mechanism secondary to the driving force produced by active salt reabsorption.

nodes.description: The primary defect in Bartter syndrome is impaired sodium chloride reabsorption in the thick ascending limb (TAL) of the loop of Henle. NKCC2 (apical) mediates electroneutral entry of Na+, K+, and 2Cl-; ROMK recycles K+ back to the lumen to sustain NKCC2 activity; and ClC-Kb (with barttin) provides basolateral Cl- exit. Loss of any component disrupts the entire transcellular NaCl transport pathway.
nodes.id: Impaired NaCl Reabsorption in Thick Ascending Limb

evidence.snippet: The main ion channel at the luminal membrane is NKCC2 (furosemide-sensitive sodium–potassium–chloride cotransporter), which belongs to a family of sodium-coupled electrolyte transporters, encoded by the solute carrier family of SLC12A genes. NKCC2 is responsible for carrying sodium from the tubular lumen into the cell coupled with potassium and chloride.

nodes.description: NKCC2-mediated K+ recycling through ROMK generates a lumen-positive transepithelial voltage in the TAL that drives paracellular reabsorption of Ca2+ and Mg2+. Loss of this voltage in Types 1 and 2 causes hypercalciuria (leading to nephrocalcinosis) and hypomagnesemia.

evidence.snippet: Henle’s loop has another important role in the reabsorption of divalent cations such as calcium (Ca2+) and magnesium (Mg2+), which are reabsorbed by a passive paracellular mechanism secondary to the driving force produced by active salt reabsorption.

nodes.description: Elevated plasma renin activity due to chronic volume contraction and macula densa sensing of reduced luminal chloride.

evidence.snippet: patients with BS present hyperreninemic hyperaldosteronism with normal/low blood pressure

nodes.description: Barttin is co-expressed with ClC-Ka and ClC-Kb in the stria vascularis of the inner ear, where chloride channels are essential for endolymph potassium secretion. Loss of barttin (Type 4A) or both ClC-K channels (Type 4B) disrupts the endocochlear potential, causing sensorineural hearing loss.

evidence.snippet: In type IVa, barttin mutations impair potassium secretion in the stria vascularis and the vestibular labyrinth, whereas in type IVb mutations they occur in both chloride channels impairing their normal function in the inner ear.

nodes.description: NaCl reabsorption in the TAL is essential for generating the corticomedullary osmotic gradient required for urinary concentration. Impaired TAL transport reduces medullary tonicity, causing nephrogenic diabetes insipidus-like polyuria, polydipsia, and isosthenuria.

evidence.snippet: Transepithelial salt movement into the medullary inter-stitium, together with the low permeability of the luminal membrane of the TAL to water, is important to allow the kidneys to develop interstitial hypertonicity and support the urinary concentrating ability.

nodes.description: Hypokalemia-related skeletal muscle weakness, which can range from mild fatigue to severe episodic paralysis.

evidence.snippet: a 26-year-old man who presented with generalized body weakness and hypokalemia and was ultimately diagnosed with Bartter syndrome type II

nodes.description: Medullary calcium deposition from chronic hypercalciuria. Most common in Types 1 and 2; less frequent in Type 3.

evidence.snippet: Nephrocalcinosis was constant in KCNJ1 and SLC12A1 but not in BSND and CLCNKB patients.

nodes.description: Compensatory increased fluid intake in response to polyuria and volume depletion.

evidence.snippet: Transepithelial salt movement into the medullary inter-stitium, together with the low permeability of the luminal membrane of the TAL to water, is important to allow the kidneys to develop interstitial hypertonicity and support the urinary concentrating ability.

nodes.description: Fetal polyuria from salt wasting causes polyhydramnios, typically presenting in the second or third trimester. Characteristic of antenatal forms (Types 1, 2, 4, 5).

evidence.snippet: The onset of polyhydramnios, birth term, height and weight were similar for all groups

nodes.description: Impaired urinary concentration due to loss of the medullary osmotic gradient. Can be severe and lead to dehydration.

evidence.snippet: BS is characterized by polyuria, failure to thrive, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, hyperreninemia, and hyperaldosteronism.

nodes.description: Polyhydramnios leads to premature labor and delivery, a major source of morbidity in antenatal Bartter syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Antenatal Bartter syndrome is characterized by severe polyhydramnios in mother leading to premature delivery.

nodes.description: Hypokalemia and volume depletion stimulate renal prostaglandin E2 synthesis, which further inhibits NaCl reabsorption in the TAL and stimulates renin release, creating a positive feedback loop that amplifies salt wasting and electrolyte disturbances.

evidence.snippet: This impaired entry of sodium and chloride into the macula densa increases the expression of cyclooxygenase-2 (COX2) stimulating the renal production of prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), also resulting in afferent arteriolar dilatation and activation of renin release by JGA.

nodes.description: Chronic volume depletion from renal salt wasting stimulates the juxtaglomerular apparatus to increase renin secretion, resulting in hyperreninemia and secondary hyperaldosteronism. Despite elevated aldosterone, blood pressure remains normal or low because the primary renal tubular defect prevents effective sodium retention.

evidence.snippet: In patients with BS, reduced reabsorption of chloride occurs due to the genetic defects, and an increase in chloride delivery to the macula densa with an abnormal volume sensing ensues. Therefore, the control of filtration becomes uncoupled from volume status.

nodes.description: Elevated aldosterone levels driven by hyperreninemia, contributing to potassium wasting and metabolic alkalosis.

evidence.snippet: BS is characterized by polyuria, failure to thrive, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, hyperreninemia, and hyperaldosteronism.

nodes.description: Bilateral sensorineural deafness resulting from impaired chloride transport in the stria vascularis of the inner ear. Specific to Type 4 (barttin or digenic ClC-Ka/ClC-Kb mutations).

evidence.snippet: Deafness at diagnosis was constant in patients with BSND mutations

type IVb is a digenic disorder with mutations in both CLCNKA and CLCNKB genes, which can lead to impairment in the functioning of two chloride channels and as a result, severe salt wasting and deafness.

nodes.description: Growth retardation is common, particularly when diagnosis and treatment are delayed. In a Korean multicenter study, 41% had short stature at last follow-up.

evidence.snippet: At the last follow-up of 8 years after the initial diagnosis, 41% had short stature (height less than 3rd percentile)

nodes.description: Chronic hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and neurohormonal abnormalities can produce clinically important cardiac arrhythmias in Bartter syndrome despite the usual absence of hypertension. Severe events may include syncope or sudden cardiac death.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiovascular and arrhythmic manifestations of Bartter's and Gitelman's syndromes: do not forget the heart. A narrative literature review.
evidence.snippet: Although considered benign entities, major adverse cardiovascular events may complicate both syndromes, in form of ventricular arrhythmias leading to palpitations, syncope or sudden cardiac death, microvascular cardiac dysfunction and exercise-induced myocardial contractile deficit.
